scene_title: London. An ante-chamber in the King's palace

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: My lord, I’ll tell you; that self bill is urged,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which in the eleventh year of the last king’s reign

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Was like, and had indeed against us pass’d,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: But that the scambling and unquiet time

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Did push it out of farther question.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: But how, my lord, shall we resist it now?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: It must be thought on. If it pass against us,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: We lose the better half of our possession:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: For all the temporal lands which men devout

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: By testament have given to the church

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Would they strip from us; being valued thus:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As much as would maintain, to the King’s honour,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Full fifteen earls and fifteen hundred knights,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Six thousand and two hundred good esquires;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And, to relief of lazars and weak age,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of indigent faint souls past corporal toil,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: A hundred almshouses right well supplied;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And to the coffers of the King beside,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: A thousand pounds by the year: thus runs the bill.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: This would drink deep.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: ’Twould drink the cup and all.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: But what prevention?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The King is full of grace and fair regard.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: And a true lover of the holy church.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The courses of his youth promised it not.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The breath no sooner left his father’s body,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: But that his wildness, mortified in him,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Seem’d to die too; yea, at that very moment

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Consideration, like an angel, came

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And whipped the offending Adam out of him,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Leaving his body as a paradise,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To envelope and contain celestial spirits.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Never was such a sudden scholar made;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Never came reformation in a flood,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: With such a heady currance, scouring faults;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Nor never Hydra-headed wilfulness

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: So soon did lose his seat and all at once

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As in this king.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: We are blessed in the change.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Hear him but reason in divinity,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And, all-admiring with an inward wish

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: You would desire the king were made a prelate:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Hear him debate of commonwealth affairs,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: You would say it hath been all in all his study:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: List his discourse of war, and you shall hear

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: A fearful battle render’d you in music:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Turn him to any cause of policy,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The Gordian knot of it he will unloose,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Familiar as his garter: that, when he speaks,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The air, a charter’d libertine, is still,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And the mute wonder lurketh in men’s ears,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To steal his sweet and honey’d sentences;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: So that the art and practic part of life

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Must be the mistress to this theoric:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which is a wonder how his grace should glean it,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Since his addiction was to courses vain,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: His companies unletter’d, rude and shallow,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: His hours fill’d up with riots, banquets sports,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And never noted in him any study,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Any retirement, any sequestration

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: From open haunts and popularity.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: The strawberry grows underneath the nettle

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: And wholesome berries thrive and ripen best

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Neighbour’d by fruit of baser quality:

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: And so the prince obscured his contemplation

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Under the veil of wildness; which, no doubt,

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Grew like the summer grass, fastest by night,

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Unseen, yet crescive in his faculty.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: It must be so; for miracles are ceased;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And therefore we must needs admit the means

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: How things are perfected.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: But, my good lord,

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: How now for mitigation of this bill

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Urged by the commons? Doth his majesty

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Incline to it, or no?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: He seems indifferent,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Or rather swaying more upon our part

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Than cherishing the exhibitors against us;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: For I have made an offer to his majesty,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Upon our spiritual convocation

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And in regard of causes now in hand,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which I have open’d to his grace at large,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As touching France, to give a greater sum

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Than ever at one time the clergy yet

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Did to his predecessors part withal.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: How did this offer seem received, my lord?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: With good acceptance of his majesty;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Save that there was not time enough to hear,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As I perceived his grace would fain have done,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The severals and unhidden passages

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of his true titles to some certain dukedoms

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And generally to the crown and seat of France

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Derived from Edward, his great-grandfather.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: What was the impediment that broke this off?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The French ambassador upon that instant

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Craved audience; and the hour, I think, is come

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To give him hearing: is it four o’clock?

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: It is.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Then go we in, to know his embassy;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which I could with a ready guess declare,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Before the Frenchman speak a word of it.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: I’ll wait upon you, and I long to hear it.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Archbishop of Canterbury, and the Bishop of Ely.

glosses.definition: same bill — a parliamentary measure that would strip the Church of lands
secular holdings — Church-owned estates granted to the Crown

glosses.phrase: true lover of the Holy Church
glosses.definition: a sincere defender of the Church — part of the play's early portrait of a reformed Henry
a wilfulness with many heads like the Lernaean Hydra — stubbornness that grows back when cut off
famously intricate knot cut by Alexander the Great — an image of a problem solved by force rather than untangling
growing through its own capacity — the medieval idea that souls develop by their own principle

annotations.title: The bill that starts the war
annotations.body: The scene opens on a parliamentary threat: a bill, first proposed in the reign of Henry IV and now revived, would strip the English Church of temporal lands worth more than half its possessions. Canterbury's calculation is precise — the confiscated revenues would maintain fifteen earls, fifteen hundred knights, six thousand two hundred esquires, a hundred almshouses, and would put £1,000 a year into the royal treasury. These figures are drawn from Raphael Holinshed's Chronicles (1587), Shakespeare's principal source. The historical bill, first raised in 1404 and revived in 1410 and 1414, was real, and so was the political logic: an ambitious Church-stripping measure was a durable threat to clerical wealth. Canterbury's counter-move — to engage the king's ambition so thoroughly in a French war that the bill cannot reach a vote — reads as realpolitik. Shakespeare shows the Church actively procuring the war. This is a materially more compromising portrait of the invasion's origins than anything Henry himself will admit in the scenes that follow.
sources: Raphael Holinshed, Chronicles of England, Scotland, and Ireland (1587)
Gary Taylor (ed.), The Oxford Shakespeare: Henry V (1982), introduction

annotations.title: The conversion narrative — Hal to Henry
annotations.body: Canterbury's account of Henry's transformation — wildness that died with his father, Consideration whipping 'the offending Adam' out of him, the king now full of divinity, policy, and theology — compresses the arc of the Henry IV plays into forty lines. It is also strategically self-serving: Canterbury is flattering the king in advance of asking him to hear the Church's war-argument. Whether Henry's piety is real or performed is one of the play's open questions. The scene leaves it ambiguous on purpose: Canterbury's 'miracle' rhetoric sits beside Ely's more coolly political observation that they still need to act to prevent the bill. Audiences who have read the Henry IV plays know that Prince Hal's transformation at his coronation was public theatre; audiences who haven't hear Canterbury's speech as sincere wonder. Both readings remain available.

annotations.title: Canterbury's motive, made explicit
annotations.body: The scene's most important structural feature is that Canterbury lays out his plan to divert the king before the king appears. By the time Henry enters in 1.2 and solicits Canterbury's legal judgement on the French claim, we already know the Archbishop has an institutional interest in encouraging the war. This changes what we hear in the next scene. When Canterbury speaks for forty lines about the Salic Law, we are not watching a disinterested legal consultation; we are watching a bought advocate. Shakespeare does not belabour the point, but he stages the causal sequence clearly: Church threat, political maneuver, war-justification. The play's later emphasis on Henry's providentialism and the God-fought nature of Agincourt must be read against this opening frame. The fact that the soldiers themselves debate the war's justice in 4.1 (Williams's challenge) is thus not a late innovation; the play has been keeping the question open from its first scene.
sources: A. R. Humphreys, The Arden Shakespeare: Henry V (1968)
Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)

trials.prompt: What is the bill that Canterbury fears (lines 1–19)?
options: A law that would ban English bishops from holding office
A parliamentary bill that would strip the Church of temporal lands worth more than half its wealth
A tax on monastic buildings only
A requirement that the Church pay for royal wars

trials.prompt: Why does Canterbury encourage Henry's war on France?
options: He believes Henry's claim is legally irrefutable, regardless of self-interest
Because the war will absorb the king's attention and political capital, preventing the bill from passing — he has an institutional motive
He personally hates the French King
Because Joan of Arc is expected to lead a counter-invasion

trials.prompt: Canterbury describes Henry's transformation from 'wildness' to kingly virtue. What dramatic history does this summary compress?
options: Prince Hal's development in Shakespeare's earlier Henry IV Parts 1 and 2
Henry III's relationship with the Church

trials.prompt: How does 1.1 frame what happens in 1.2?
options: It establishes that Canterbury has a material interest in promoting the French war BEFORE he is asked to legally justify it — the audience hears the next scene's legal opinion as compromised
It sets up a love plot that the rest of the play will develop
